title: Extract out timelock manager
body:
After giving some thinking about the transfer service that is too large IMO my conclusion is that it attempts to cover too much functionality in one service without proper separation of concerns which makes it hard to reason about the code on every debugging session.

Sharing my thoughts here about the future of it to get some feedback.
Seems to me the transfer service should be composed of roughly these decoupled components:
* transfer_sender - for sending transfer
* transfer_claimer - for claiming transfer
* timelock_manager - for refreshing/extending timelocks

Seems also that key tweaking and signing can be extracted out to a utilities to be used from the above components.

This obviously  needs to be done gradually so I started with the `timelock_manager` where I just extracted out the relevant methods out of the transfer service.
The idea is that it will only be used from the TreeService  (before or after the user operation) and remove it from the transfer service.

title: Allow concurrent leaves operations
body:
Allow concurrent operations by reserving leaves for each operation. A reservation gets the leaves out of the main pool and put them in the reserve pool.

Then the caller has two choices:
1. Cancel the reservation - Should be called when the operation fails which returns the leaves to the main pool
2. Finalize the reservation - Should be called when the operation succeed which removes the leaves from the reserve pool.

In addition this PR removes all occurrences to refresh_leaves after specific operations as instead we add/remove leaves directly to the tree state resulting in faster time.

title: Concurrent tree service
body:
This update introduces support for concurrent operations by introducing a leaf reservation mechanism.

Each operation (e.g., Lightning payments, transfers, swaps, etc.) begins by reserving a set of leaves. Reserved leaves are moved from the main pool into a reserved pool, effectively "locking" them for that specific operation and making them unavailable to others.

Once the operation is complete, the caller has two options:

Cancel the reservation – Used when the operation fails. This returns the leaves to the main pool.

Finalize the reservation – Used when the operation succeeds. This removes the leaves from the reserved pool.

Additional changes in this PR:
Removed all refresh_leaves calls after specific operations. Instead, leaves are now added or removed directly from the tree state, resulting in improved performance.

Removed timelock_manager from the wallet. Time lock logic is now fully encapsulated within the tree_service, where leaf selection, insertion, and refreshing are managed centrally.

title: Updates for unilateral exit
body:
Updates the transaction creation to support user and watchtower initiated unilateral exits.

Node creation generates two types of transactions:
1. A CPFP (Child Pays For Parent) transaction that always includes an anchor output for fee bumping
2. An optional direct transaction that can be used for direct spending

Refund creation generates three possible transactions:
1. A CPFP (Child Pays For Parent) transaction that always includes an anchor output
2. An optional direct transaction that spends from the direct node outpoint
3. An optional direct transaction that spends from the CPFP outpoint, but with a different sequence number

The CPFP root, branch and leaf node transactions are to be broadcast by the user in case of a unilateral exit, along which the CPFP leaf refund transaction.

The direct node and refund transactions are to be used by the watchtower to be broadcast on the user's behalf if in case of an attack while the user is offline and unable to broadcast the CPFP transactions themselves. The sequence number for the direct transactions are always `DIRECT_TIME_LOCK_OFFSET` blocks higher than the CPFP transactions so that the CPFP transactions can be broadcast first.

```
                            +---------------+
                            | Deposit TX    |
                            | (On-chain)    |
                            +-------+-------+
                                    |
                      +-------------+--------------+
                      |                            |
            +---------v----------+       +---------v----------+
            | CPFP Root TX       |       | Direct Root TX     |
            | (anchor, no fee)   |       | (no anchor, fee)   |
            +---------+----------+       +---------+----------+
                      |                            |
       +--------------+-------------+              +----------+
       |                            |                         |
  +----v-------------+      +-------v----------+       +------v-----------+
  | CPFP Refund TX   |      | Direct From CPFP |       | Direct Refund TX |
  | (anchor, no fee) |      | Refund TX        |       | (no anchor, fee) |
  |                  |      | (no anchor, fee) |       |                  |
  +------------------+      +------------------+       +------------------+
```

https://docs.spark.money/wallet/developer-guide/unilateral-exit

This PR is an initial step towards supporting Wasm. It includes:

- Fixing Wasm compilation/clippy errors
- Running tests on Wasm (browser + node.js)

Additionally, because I was seeing that our makefiles were getting very difficult to track, and it got even worse with supporting wasm targets, I propose we follow the [xtasks pattern](https://github.com/matklad/cargo-xtask) and drop all makefiles except the base one. This allows running all tasks from a central place and help a lot with avoiding task definition repetition. We can still run tasks (e.g. test or wasm test) on individual crates. Some example usage:

- `cargo run -p xtask -- test` runs all non wasm tests
- `cargo run -p xtask -- test -p  -- --ignored` runs non wasm ignored tests for crate ``
- `cargo run -p xtask -- wasm-test --node` runs all wasm tests on node.js
- `cargo run -p xtask -- clippy --fix` runs clippy (applying fixes) over the entire workspace

title: retry transport errors with custom retry channel
body:
After a phone goes to sleep, when the phone wakes up again, the first grpc request after that will fail with a transport error. In order to ensure that no retry logic is needed on every request, a retry channel is introduced that does the retrying for us. This way every grpc request executes reliably in this scenario.

The implementation seems quite hacky, but it works. 

Hacks:
- reconstructing the request by reading the buffer
- polling the inner service in the call function
- either using a mutex or cloning the inner service to do the calls (see the difference between the two commits)

I think making a clone of the inner service is better than using a mutex, because channels should be cheap to clone.

Test by:
- running the cli
- changing network
- doing a command that interacts with the server, like `sync`.
- the retry channel should retry the request and succeed the second time.

As part of this I refactored a bit the way we prepare and send payments.
The advantage I see in the new approach is:
1. We have the ability to return in the prepare response data related to the specific payment without adding optional fields on the top level
2. Due to 1 we can now support more complex payment requests (such as lightning address, bip21) as we can return the various payment methods to the user in the prepare step.
3. The send step now contains optional SendOptions field that should contain specific data for the specific payment (such as prefer_spark, selected fees for send on chain, etc...).

More updates:
* unified sync ans deposit loop and use sync_interval_seconds instead of deposit_syc_interval.
* Make PaymendDetails optional on Payment and add PaymentMethod on payment. Reason is because it turns out sometimes we don't get that info from spark and then we couldn't identify the actual payment method.

title: simplify deposit management
body:
Added some simplifications:
1. One table unclaimed_deposits for those with claim error or refund_tx
2. Added tx downloader that uses the storage for cache (to download tx only once).
3. Simplify deposit related storage functions to be clearer API wise.

The deposit claim flow goes like this:
1. Every sync we check for unclaimed deposits.
2. The DepositSyncer runs and its job is to synchronize the deposits in the storage so every deposit can be in one of the following states:

- Unclaimed - just waiting to be claimed
- Unclaimed with error - at least one claim attempt was made which has failed
- Refunded - User initiated refund, tx is still not confirmed.

Once either claimed or refund confirmed the deposit is removed from the storage as we no longer track it. (the part of tracking refund tx is not implemented in this PR).

title: Claim pending transfer on event stream reconnection
body:
This fixes an issue where, if a transfer is received while the stream is disconnected, we would miss the transfer event and not claim it (it would only be claimed on restart). Now, we attempt to claim pending transfers whenever we reconnect to the stream, ensuring that we gracefully handle any missed events.

Previously, we stored the IDs of transfers claimed on startup to ensure we didn't attempt to claim them again if we received an event for them. Now there's no protection against it. In my tests, I never received an event for a transfer that was received while offline or disconnected; however, if this does happen, we shouldn't encounter any issues. The background processor processes events sequentially, so it won't ever try to claim the same transfer concurrently. It may try it sequentially, but we should get a harmless error about the transfer already having been claimed.

**Misc**: this also includes some logging improvements:
- Not logging complete transfers and other big types on debug/info, just on trace level
- More expressive logging in `claim_pending_transfers`. Once I faced a weird situation where it hanged, but I haven't been able to reproduce it again yet. The logging will be helpful if we reencounter it.

title: lnurl server
body:
lnurl server implementation.

A user can register for a lightning address / lnurl pay link. Using a pubkey, username and description (for the lnurl metadata).
Then the lnurl server can create invoices on the user's behalf.

This PR contains the lnurl server, the client implementation has to be made as well. When building the client implementation I'll see if there is need to store the created invoice with preimage and transfer id or not. 

Using `diesel` for the database schema. The idea was that we would be able to swap sqlite for postgres easily. However, that is not the case unfortunately. At least it's not as easy as using some generics. There will have to be two implementations for the different databases, even though the code accessing them would be quite the same. I'm thinking it might be good to switch to `sqlx` instead, because that has async support. Currently the server only supports sqlite.

The server now uses the `verify_message` to verify the signed payload. Note that to sign you will not use the identity key, but a leaf pubkey. So you won't be able to use the equivalent `sign_message` for that.

title: Tree service cleanup
body:
Starting to address #206 
This PR cleanup the TreeService:
1. Move `collect_leaves` to the deposit service where it is only used. By that also makes the deposit service covers all deposit flow.
2. Move the `transfer_leaves_to_self` to the transfer service (now uses internally `transfer_leaves_to`)
3. Better encapsulate lightning payments inside lightning service (remove friction from the wallet).

Everything in Spark ultimately maps to a Transfer, which suggests that the TransferService operates at a lower level than higher-level services like Deposit or Lightning. The idea is for these higher-level services to use the TransferService internally, allowing them to encapsulate the entire flow rather than use the wallet as facade that adds friction. 
This way, SparkWallet only needs to handle the final results (ideally reserving leaves only, responding to events, etc...) and can delegate the detailed processes to the services themselves.

Wishful thinking: I believe the flakiness issue with the deposit integration tests might also be resolved.

Modular tree service
body:
This PR extracts the TreeService and TreeState (renamed to TreeStore) into two separate traits.
By doing so, Spark Wallet can now work with these abstractions, paving the way for users to provide their own custom store or service implementations when they need maximum control (note: passing custom implementations is not yet supported in this PR).

This design is particularly useful in service-oriented architectures, where multiple clients or workers need access to the same tree state. In such scenarios, the tree can be managed as a shared state service, accessible to all clients either synchronously or asynchronously.

Additionally, the selection logic has been moved into the store itself, rather than residing in the service. This shift enables different store implementations (e.g., Redis, SQL) to leverage their native querying capabilities for selection, instead of requiring all leaves to be loaded into memory.

Some static selection utils were extracted out to a selection_helper.rs file.

Sync head and tail using Sparkscan API
body:
Splits `sync_payments_to_storage()` fn  into `sync_payments_head_to_storage()` and `sync_payments_tail_to_storage()` so it can sync both the head and tail.

The first head sync syncs 1 page then lets the tail sync do the rest. The following head syncs will attempt to sync until the `last_synced_payment_id` is found. If we get an error from the Sparkscan API (maybe hit a request limit), we store what payments we have and cache the `next_head_offset` to start the head sync from in the next cycle.

The tail sync syncs a maximum of 5 pages per cycle, starting at an offset counting the stored completed payments. Once there are no more pages available, we set the `tail_synced` and won't try to sync it again.

**Update**: This also temporarily disables using Sparkscan to sync and reverts to using @danielgranhao's bitcoin/token sync

title: Better balance management
body:
This PR addresses two areas:

**Payments at startup (pre-first sync)**

We now retry leaf reservation by fetching the latest state from Spark when a reservation fails. Since the cached tree may be stale, the flow is:
Attempt reservation optimistically using the cached tree.
On failure, refresh state; if potential balance suffices, retry (up to 3 times).

**Accurate balance when some leaves are missing on certain operators**

Balance is now partitioned into four disjoint groups:
* available_leaves — leaves currently eligible for reservation
* non_available_leaves — leaves whose status ≠ TreeNode::Available
* reserved_leaves — leaves already reserved by an operation
* missing_operators_leaves — leaves not present on all operators

“Correct” balance is computed as:
available_leaves + missing_operators_leaves
since missing operator presence is treated as a temporary condition.

title: gracefully handle already claimed transfers
body:
A common case is that a transfer has already been claimed by another instance of the sdk, or even the same instance, in another flow. Currently the claim_transfer function then errors. This can leave payments in a pending state until the next sync round.

In order to fix this, we'll gracefully handle already claimed incoming transfers. If there is an error from the operator, we'll check what the state of the transfer is on the operator, by querying the transfer and determining its state. If the state is already past the expected state, we'll return OK. The TransferAlreadyClaimed error is now handled inside claim_transfer, to minimize the amount of code paths that have to handle this error.

Fixes #282

title: Replace refresh/extend with renew leaf flow
body:
Replaces the refresh/extend timelock flow with renew node/refund/zero timelock flows.
- On each transfer a leaf's refund tx sequence decremented
- When the refund tx sequence is <= 100 it calls renew_refund, which decrements the node tx sequence and resets the refund tx sequence
- When the node tx sequence is <= 100 it calls renew_node, which splits off zero timelock node txs. From these split parent txs resets the node and refund tx sequences
- If a node tx sequence is already at 0, it can't be decremented further. In this case renew_zero_timeout create a new zero timelock node tx and resets the refund tx sequence

Closes #202

title: Real-time sync
body:
This PR adds real-time sync functionality.
Only the payment metadata is synchronized currently.

I did my best to create a proper separation of concerns. The sync is completely standalone, outside the domain of the relational data model. I wanted to put all that code in the `common` crate, but I didn't want to create a new persister trait for this. So some code is in the `core` package because of that.

The only part of the code that knows about sync is the wrapper repository around our existing repository. It calls the sync service to set a record _before_ (that is important) inserting in the relational data model. It also listens to incoming changes through a callback mechanism. This wrapper repository would be the only thing needed in the core package if persistence was in common.

The sync basically works like this:
Outgoing changes:
- first insert into outgoing_sync, then in relational data model. If we would do that the other way around, you would not be able to track the change anymore.
- In the background these changes are pushed to remote.
- After pushing they are removed from the outgoing table in the same transaction as they are inserted in the sync state.

Incoming changes:
- First they are inserted into an incoming table
- Then the pending outgoing changes are 'rebased'. Currently they only need a new revision number. In the future 'rebase' could mean more complex logic.
- Then the incoming change is inserted in the sync state. Note the incoming change is not deleted yet, so we know there is a pending change.
- Then the callback is invoked to update the relational data model. 
- Only then is the incoming change deleted.

These sequences of events ensure consistency at all times. Just the order is important.

title: client side zaps
body:
This PR adds zap functionality if privacy mode is enabled. It also enables lnurl comments up to 255 characters. In a nutshell it works as follows
- client side registers nostr pubkey along with lightning address
- client synchronizes invoice metadata from lnurl server when receiving a payment with a description hash, and periodically.
- in the background, client publishes zap receipts where necessary. Zap receipts are published through the lnurl server, so they don't have to be real-time synced.

Other comments
- I changed the way sync works a bit. Parallelizing it, and making it so you can more precisely set which things to sync by using bitflags.
- the nostr pubkey is set in the register lightning address request based on the privacy setting on the operator. This means before registering the lightning address, we now query the operator for the privacy setting.
- the nostr pubkey is derived from the seed, according to NIP-06 (I think)
- the sender comment and the nostr zap request are immediately available when the PaymentSucceeded event is published. The zap receipt is available later, after it is published.

title: Leaf optimization options and control
body:
Closes #463 and #464.

Changes:
- Adds a tool crate to benchmark payment times
- Optimization can now be configured, allowing swaps to be minimized instead of just optimizing for max unilateral exit amount. Optimizing for max unilateral exit is still possible (multiplicity = 0)
- Automatic optimization can be disabled. Wallet exposes methods to start and cancel optimization.
- Trying to pay cancels an ongoing optimization if not enough leaves are available
- Optimization is done in rounds (configurable size), and as each round completes, output leaves become immediately available
- TreeService/store allows reserving leaves for swaps, the difference being that these are still included in the balance and they are not removed from the store on refresh

TODO:

- [x] Re-add retries to deal with payment attempts before sync (but now implement on wallet level)
- [x] Choose default multiplicity (need first to measure impact of different multiplicity configs on the amount that can unilaterally exit)
- [x] Reserve leaves for each round only at the round start
- [x] Docs

Some preliminary results (all values are rough approximations):
- Using original implementation (same as multiplicity = 0) -> 70% swap rate
- Multiplicity = 1 
    - 2 s between payments -> 50% swap rate
    - 20 s between payments -> 0% swap rate
    
Analysis on impact of multiplicity on unilateral exit cost: https://gist.github.com/danielgranhao/601f8e3703ca3df62b66359cf722501b
    
Payments with swaps generally take ~10 secs.
Payments without swaps generally take ~1 sec. Occasionally, take more (always less than 10s) when they need to wait for an ongoing optimization to get cancelled.

title: Implement LUD-21 LNURL-verify
body:
## Resolves #278 

## Summary

  Implements [LUD-21](https://github.com/lnurl/luds/blob/luds/21.md) which allows services to check whether a Lightning invoice has been paid by querying the LNURL server.

  **What is LUD-21?**
  When a wallet pays an LNURL invoice, the payer may want to verify that the payment was received (e.g., for proof of payment in e-commerce). LUD-21 defines a `verify` URL that returns the payment status and preimage.

  ## Changes

  ### Database Schema
  Added new table `lnurl_pay_invoices`

  ### Invoice Creation (`/lnurlp/{user}/invoice`)
  When creating an invoice, we now:
  1. Store the `res.id` from `create_lightning_invoice()` as `lightning_receive_id`
  2. Store the `res.invoice` as `bolt11_invoice`
  3. Return a `verify` URL in the response

  ### Verify Endpoint (`/lnurlp/{user}/verify?pr=lnbc...`)
  1. Parse the invoice from `pr` query param to extract `payment_hash`
  2. Look up the stored `lightning_receive_id` from the database
  3. Call `wallet.fetch_lightning_receive_payment()` to get current payment status
  4. Return LUD-21 compliant response with `settled` and `preimage`

##  Assumptions

  1. Payment is settled when preimage exists: We determine settled: true by checking if payment.payment_preimage.is_some(). This assumes Spark only populates the preimage field once the payment is fully received.
  2. Verify requires the full invoice: Per LUD-21, the verify endpoint requires the pr (invoice) as a query parameter. We parse it to extract the payment hash for lookup.
  3. Domain/username validation: We verify the invoice belongs to the user in the URL path to prevent cross-user invoice status leakage.

## Test Plan

- Create an invoice via /lnurlp/{user}/invoice?amount=10000
- Verify the response includes a verify URL
- Call verify endpoint before payment → returns {"settled": false, "preimage": null, ...}
- Pay the invoice via Lightning Network
- Call verify endpoint after payment → returns {"settled": true, "preimage": "...", ...}
- Verify that querying with an invoice from a different user returns an error
- Verify that querying with an unknown invoice returns "invoice not found"

- Fix bug that would lead to wrong balance when outputs of a certain token exceeded 500 (no pagination on token querying)
- Prevent Spark self-payments. We didn't prevent this in the past, but we didn't support it properly either. Bitcoin self-payments would result in the list containing only outgoing payments without a matching incoming payment. Token self-payments wouldn't appear as payments at all. As I don't currently see a use case for self-payments, I think it's simpler and appropriate to simply prevent them.

Notes:
- I didn't expose configuration or control of the token optimization process at the Breez SDK level. Unlike leaf optimization, token optimization has a low likelihood of affecting payments due to it being a quicker process that needs to occur less often. I propose we wait before exposing it to see if we find a compelling reason to do it.

title: fix: counter swap resulting in pending Payment
body:
If a leaf swap ( during optimization or at payment time) fails to have the counter swap transfer claimed, the counter swap will end up being claimed later in the background by `claim_pending_transfers`. 

Given that claiming all pending transfers can result in `TransferClaimed` events, we would create a Payment for the transfer and persist it. The payment would be pending because we were also missing updating the transfer status after claiming

Fixes in this PR:
- Converting a transfer into a Payment now fails if the transfer is associated with a swap (failed conversions are ignored when handling wallet events)
- We now update all claimed transfer status to `Completed`
- I noticed we would only claim these counter swap transfers when restarting the stream, so now we check for pending transfers on sync

title: Add Claude Code GitHub Workflow
body:
## 🤖 Installing Claude Code GitHub App

This PR adds a GitHub Actions workflow that enables Claude Code integration in our repository.

### What is Claude Code?

[Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) is an AI coding agent that can help with:
- Bug fixes and improvements  
- Documentation updates
- Implementing new features
- Code reviews and suggestions
- Writing tests
- And more!

### How it works

Once this PR is merged, we'll be able to interact with Claude by mentioning @claude in a pull request or issue comment.
Once the workflow is triggered, Claude will analyze the comment and surrounding context, and execute on the request in a GitHub action.

### Important Notes

- **This workflow won't take effect until this PR is merged**
- **@claude mentions won't work until after the merge is complete**
- The workflow runs automatically whenever Claude is mentioned in PR or issue comments
- Claude gets access to the entire PR or issue context including files, diffs, and previous comments

### Security

- Our Anthropic API key is securely stored as a GitHub Actions secret
- Only users with write access to the repository can trigger the workflow
- All Claude runs are stored in the GitHub Actions run history
- Claude's default tools are limited to reading/writing files and interacting with our repo by creating comments, branches, and commits.
- We can add more allowed tools by adding them to the workflow file like:

```
allowed_tools: Bash(npm install),Bash(npm run build),Bash(npm run lint),Bash(npm run test)
```

There's more information in the [Claude Code action repo](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code-action).

After merging this PR, let's try mentioning @claude in a comment on any PR to get started!

title: feat: migrate Flutter build system from Make to Just
body:
## Summary

Migrates Flutter build system from Make to Just, aligning with breez-sdk-liquid and misty-breez patterns.

## Key Changes

- **Justfile**: Created with recipes for setup, build, codegen, version, test, clean
- **Version script**: Added `scripts/version.sh` to sync version from workspace Cargo.toml to all platform files
- **CI workflow**: Added just setup, static analysis, formatting checks, and version validation
- **Publish workflow**: Migrated to just recipes, aligned with breez-sdk-liquid pattern
- **Flutter**: Upgraded to 3.38.9 (latest stable)

## Build Commands

| Old | New |
|-----|-----|
| `make generate-bindings-build-release` | `just build` |
| `make generate-bindings` | `just gen` |
| `make build-release` | `just build-release` |
| - | `just setup` |
| - | `just version` |

## Benefits

- Consistency across all Breez projects
- Automated version management prevents drift
- Better CI checks (formatting, analysis, version sync)
- Self-documenting commands via `just --list`

---

title: Drain all funds
body:
Closes #499.

Changes:
- Add drain feature to send entire Bitcoin balance
- Introduce new PayAmount enum with Bitcoin, Token, and Drain variants. For LNURL, BitcoinPayAmount is used - same but without Token variant.
- **Breaking change**: Replace amount/token_identifier fields with pay_amount in PrepareSendPaymentRequest and PrepareLnurlPayRequest

LNURL implementation notes:
- For LNURL, fee overpayment is used to make sure we get to 0 balance. A simple approach is used: we get an invoice for the total balance, check fee, then get the actual invoice we intend to pay for (balance - fee). I did some experiments, and saw fee overpayments of at most 1 sat or less than 0.01% for big amounts (e.g. 1 bitcoin). 
- I added a protection/sanity check that prevents fee overpayment amount to be higher than the needed fee. In any case, the user also reviews the total fee before committing to the drain.

title: Add Claude Code agent for automated PR reviews
body:
This PR introduces `@pr-reviewer` Claude Code agent for automated PR reviews.

It’s designed to be a step-up over the default behavior by offering more valuable, actionable reviews with high-level thinking and less verbosity (_while also being cheaper & faster_), streamline common follow-up actions for PRs, and evolve collaboratively through shared feedback and continuous iteration.

That last part is crucial, as this is where I see the most benefit from this tool. My impressions have improved immensely since I started integrating the reasoning behind some of the issues you raised in PR comments that it had previously reviewed but overlooked.

### Invocation:
- **Claude Code CLI:** `@pr-reviewer review this PR` which inherits users active model for the review.
- **GitHub UI**: Mention @claude to review a PR and add any additional context, it will automatically pick up the agent for the task.

There's also a `/review {pr-number} {--post}` command for preliminary reviews, mainly for PRs in draft stage, to be used locally. This behaves differently than an agent with skills, but it's loosely based on the same flow.
- If `{pr-number}` is not provided, the command defaults to the active PR on the current branch.
  - If current branch has no PR's, it gracefully exits.
- If `{--post}` is provided, the review is automatically posted to the PR thread.

### Review Flow (_outdated_, see [.claude/skills/pr-review/REVIEW_WORKFLOW.md](https://github.com/breez/spark-sdk/pull/554/changes#diff-97d0245b0b1c44fdcf485d69bc96b721082f7a78b387923f44c53e08c2978454)):
- Fetches the PR diff and existing discussions to avoid duplicate feedback.
- Posts overall review with suggested changes in inline comments based on `@pr-reviewer` agent workflow.
- Prompts the author if follow-up issue should be created on reference apps if bindings were touched.
  - If approved, a follow-up issue is created based on an issue template with summary of changes, example snippets & task list for integration.
- When files exceed the token limit (~25,000 tokens), the review includes a disclaimer listing affected files. This helps identify files that may benefit from refactoring/splitting, or should be added to filter-diff.sh ignore patterns (auto-generated files, lockfiles, etc.).

#### Note
If agent causes any regressions, it can be disabled through `.claude/settings.json.`
```
{
  "permissions": {
    "deny": ["Task(pr-reviewer)"]
  }
}
```

### Other Changes
- Split `CLAUDE.md` into modular rules to improve memory management by reducing always-loaded context. 
  - Path-specific rules conditional apply when Claude is working with files matching the specified patterns. Rules without a paths field are loaded unconditionally and apply to all files.
```
.claude/
├── rules/                      # paths
│   ├── architecture.md         #
│   ├── bindings.md             # "crates/breez-sdk/bindings/**/*,packages/**/*"
│   ├── claude-config.md        # ".claude/**/*"
│   ├── documentation.md        # "docs/**/*,**/*.md"
│   ├── project.md              # 
│   ├── rust.md                 # "{crates,packages/flutter/rust}/**/*.rs"
│   ├── security.md             # "**/signer/**/*,**/token/**/*,crates/spark-wallet/**/*"
│   ├── wasm.md                 # "**/wasm/**/*"
└────── CLAUDE.md               #
```
- Applied Claude 4.x prompting best practices

### References
- [Agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference#agents)
- [Skills](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference#skills)
- [Slash Commands](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/slash-commands)
- [Rules](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory#modular-rules-with-claude/rules/)
- [Prompting best practices](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/claude-4-best-practices)

##### I used [anthropics](https://github.com/anthropics) own open-sourced agents as baseline.

title: Parallelize breez-itest tests
body:
Add faucet rate limiting via semaphore to enable parallel test execution while respecting faucet API constraints. This addresses the first part of issue #548, bringing execution time in CI from ~50 to ~30 minutes.

Changes:
- Add global semaphore (FAUCET_CONCURRENCY env var, default: 2) to limit concurrent faucet requests in crates/breez-sdk/breez-itest/src/faucet.rs
- Update Makefile to run tests with 8 threads instead of 1
- Add FAUCET_CONCURRENCY env var to CI workflow

Tests that don't use the faucet (message_signing, user_settings, tokens, lnurl_auth) can now run fully in parallel. Tests that use the faucet can run in parallel but are rate-limited by the semaphore, preventing API rate limiting issues.

title: Fix critical memory leak in SendPayment that causes OOM crashes
body:
Fixed a critical event listener leak in wait_for_payment() that caused
memory to grow unbounded over 2+ hours, eventually crashing services.

The bug occurred when get_payment_by_invoice() returned an error - the
early return via ? operator prevented remove_event_listener() from being
called, leaving listeners orphaned in the EventEmitter map. Each leaked
listener held a 20-message mpsc channel and BTreeMap entry.

Solution: Wrapped the function body in an async block to ensure cleanup
always executes, even on error paths. This guarantees listeners are
removed regardless of how the function returns.

Also removed 5 unnecessary Box::pin() calls that were wrapping already-
async functions and immediately awaiting them, improving performance.

Fixes: SendPayment hanging and memory exhaustion under error conditions

title: Lock-Free Tree Store
body:
This PR replaces the mutex-based InMemoryTreeStore with a queue-based command processing pattern to address severe performance issues under load.

**Problem**
The previous implementation had significant drawbacks when handling concurrent payments:

* High lock contention: Mutex-based synchronization created bottlenecks under load
* Leaf racing:  Concurrent payments competed for the same leaves, causing excessive failed retries
* Swap inefficiency: When a reservation required a swap, leaves were added to state before re-selection, forcing the current * reserve call to race for the very leaves it just swapped, significantly reducing its chance of success

**Solution**
The new architecture introduces:

* Single-threaded state processor: All state mutations flow through an mpsc channel, eliminating mutex contention
* Balance change notifications: Watch channel enables efficient waiting, retries only occur when success is possible
* Concurrency limiting: Semaphore restricts concurrent reservations to 10 (currently hardcoded; configuration options will be exposed once the architecture stabilizes)
* In-place swap updates: Reservations are updated directly after swap rather than requiring a new reservation call

Testing
Added parallel-perf, a new parallel benchmark tool as a binary in the breez-bench crate for throughput testing.

title: feat: Add LNURL bech32 encoding support
body:
This PR adds support for the widely adopted bech32 representation of LNURLs, allowing users to access both URL and bech32 formats of the same LNURL.

The implementation introduces a new `LnurlInfo` struct that encapsulates both representations and provides a factory method for automatic encoding.

## Breaking Changes ⚠️

### API Changes
- **`LightningAddressInfo.lnurl`** field type changed from `String` to `LnurlInfo` struct
  - **Before:** `address_info.lnurl: String` (URL format only)
  - **After:** `address_info.lnurl.url: String` (URL format) and `address_info.lnurl.bech32: String` (bech32 format)
  - **Migration:** Users accessing the LNURL URL must update `address_info.lnurl` → `address_info.lnurl.url`

### Affected APIs
- Rust SDK: `BreezSdk::register_lightning_address()` and `BreezSdk::recover_lightning_address()`
- UniFFI, WASM & Flutter bindings: `LightningAddressInfo` type

## Changes

### Core Implementation
- Added `encode_lnurl_to_bech32()` utility function in `breez-sdk-common/src/lnurl/mod.rs` to convert LNURL URLs to bech32 format using the 'lnurl' HRP
- Created `LnurlInfo` struct with `url` and `bech32` fields to provide semantic grouping of both LNURL representations
- Added `LnurlInfo::new()` factory method that automatically encodes the bech32 format from a URL

### API Updates
- Refactored `LightningAddressInfo` to use nested `LnurlInfo` struct
- Updated `From` implementation & `register_lightning_address()` SDK method to use the new factory method

title: use greedy algorithm for leaf selection
body:
The existing algorithm for leaf selection uses dynamic programming to find a set of leaves matching the target value. In the worst case, however, the memory consumption for this was proportional to the target amount. So for example a target amount of 1M sat could use approximately 1M * ~32 bytes = 32MB of memory.

We replace the existing algorithm with a simple greedy selection algorithm. It simply takes the largest value first, and then the lower, etc. This will work most of the time, because leaves are generally powers of two. In the case non-power-of-two leaves exist, swaps will be performed more often than previously, but we think this is an edge case. The memory consumption was so large, that tradeoff is acceptable.

2 integration tests were added to check whether the algorithm wrks correctly even with non-power-of-two denominations. 1 of these integration tests is disabled, however. It waits for #580.

title: fix(sync): make initial payment metadata sync retry-safe.
body:
- Prevent setting sync completion marker before work is done
- Add in-progress marker to avoid concurrent executions
- Clear in-progress marker on failure to allow retry
- Only mark as complete after all payment metadata is successfully synced

Previously, the INITIAL_SYNC_CACHE_KEY was set before processing
payments, which could leave the system in a permanently broken state
if any error occurred during sync. Now completion is only marked
after successful execution.

The fix includes:
1. Checking for existing completion before starting
2. Setting an in-progress marker to prevent concurrent runs  
3. Only setting completion marker after successful sync
4. Clearing in-progress marker on any error

title: spark-wallet: Stop background tasks on SDK disconnect
body:
The BackgroundProcessor in SparkWallet (running token output optimization and event subscription) continued running after disconnect() was called. This occurred because the wallet created its own internal cancellation channel that only triggered on drop, while BreezSdk::disconnect() signaled a separate channel the wallet didn't observe. The Arc references held by background tasks prevented the wallet from being dropped.

The fix adds WalletBuilder::with_cancellation_token() to pass the SDK's shutdown signal to the wallet. When provided, background tasks observe this external token; otherwise, the original drop-based behavior is preserved for standalone usage.

This was observed using Wasm where the backgrounds tasks kept running after `sdk.disconnect(); sdk = null`. This might also happen in the UniFFI bindings until garbage collection occurs.

title: Fix race condition in lnurl receive metadata sync
body:
Closes #531

## Summary
Fixed a race condition in the payment sync logic where metadata sync could be skipped if a payment existed in storage but its lnurl receive metadata hadn't been synced yet from the `TransferClaimStarting` event.

## Changes
- Updated the early return condition in `BreezSdk::sync()` to only skip metadata sync when metadata is actually present (`Some(_)`)
- Previously, the code would return early even when `db_lnurl_receive_metadata` was `None`, preventing necessary metadata synchronization
- Added clarifying comments explaining the race condition and the fix

## Implementation Details
The fix uses a pattern match guard (`@ Some(_)`) to ensure we only return early if metadata has already been synced. This prevents a scenario where:
1. A payment is stored in the database
2. But the metadata sync from `TransferClaimStarting` hasn't completed yet
3. The old code would skip the sync, leaving the payment with incomplete metadata

This ensures metadata synchronization is always triggered when needed, even if the payment record already exists in storage.

body:

## Key Changes
- Added `storage` clone to the payment polling task in `BreezSdk::poll_lightning_send_payment`
- Insert the completed payment into storage before emitting the `SdkEvent` to clients

## Implementation Details
The fix addresses a race condition where clients receiving the payment completion event could call `get_payment` and receive stale data. By persisting the payment status to storage before emitting the event, we guarantee that subsequent queries will return the updated payment state.

Error handling is defensive - if storage insertion fails, we log the error but still emit the event to ensure clients are notified of the completion.

title: rtsync: defer unsupported records
body:
## Summary
Make real-time sync forward-compatible across client versions by deferring unsupported records instead of failing, and decouple pending-outgoing ordering from server revisions.

## What changed
- Added schema compatibility checks (`SchemaVersion::is_supported_by`) and explicit processing outcomes (`RecordOutcome`, `OutgoingPrepareOutcome`).
- Updated sync processor flow to:
  - keep unsupported/unknown incoming records in `sync_incoming` for retry
  - prepare outgoing records via `prepare_outgoing_push` before pushing (the idea is that later we can use this to implement proper rebasing/upgrade of old outgoing updates that were deferred)
  - push only records marked `Ready`,
  - treat startup local-pull errors as non-fatal and retry in loop/backoff.
- Replaced pending outgoing `revision` semantics with `local_revision` (queue id) across common/core/wasm models.
- Removed `rebase_pending_outgoing_records` from storage traits and implementations (Rust + WASM JS interface).
- Updated SQLite/Postgres/IndexedDB/Node storage logic to generate outgoing ids from `sync_outgoing` only

title: Concurrency optimizations
body:
This PR introduces several changes aimed at enabling backend deployments to handle high-concurrency workloads more effectively.

The primary factors impacting high concurrency, in order of significance, are:

**Leaves distribution**
To support N concurrent requests, the system must be able to reserve N distinct combinations of leaves simultaneously. This requires support for higher multiplicity in leaf reservations.

**Swap rate**
Our goal is to minimize swaps and maximize “exact-match” reservations. Achieving this requires a background optimizer that continuously distributes leaves in a way that maximizes the probability of matching arbitrary amounts. This optimizer already existed prior to this PR. The change introduced here ensures that optimization-related reservation attempts are non-blocking and return immediately if a reservation cannot be made, skipping optimization and prioritizing payment throughput.

**Rate limiting and service concurrency**
There is a limit to the number of concurrent requests due to throttling and rate limits on the Spark side. To mitigate this, we enable multiple workers to run concurrently against the same TreeStore service. Exposing a PostgreSQL-backed TreeStore is out of scope for this PR.
A change was made to handle rate limit error better and retry with backoff. 

Numbers:
Running the perf tool I get between 8-20 TPS depends on the swap rate.

For example this run will get 16 TPS as almost no swaps are executed:
```
cargo run --bin parallel-perf -- --pre-optimize 80 --transfers 60 --lightning 0  --delay-ms 5 --min-amount 1 --max-amount 1

All payments completed in 3.73s

============================================================
SUMMARY
============================================================
Total payments: 60 (60 transfers + 0 lightning)
Success rate: 60/60 (100.0%)
Throughput: 965.4 payments/minute

Duration Statistics (successful payments):
  Min: 625ms   Max: 3.38s   Mean: 1.38s
  p50: 1.12s   p95: 2.62s   p99: 3.35s

Transfer Statistics (n=60):
  Min: 625ms   Max: 3.38s   Mean: 1.38s
  p50: 1.12s   p95: 2.62s   p99: 3.35s
```


The following results in ~9.5 TPS as at one point most of the payments use swaps:

```
cargo run --bin parallel-perf -- --pre-optimize 10 --transfers 300 --lightning 0  --delay-ms 5 --min-amount 1 --max-amount 1

============================================================
SUMMARY
============================================================
Total payments: 300 (300 transfers + 0 lightning)
Success rate: 300/300 (100.0%)
Throughput: 568.2 payments/minute

Duration Statistics (successful payments):
  Min: 650ms   Max: 22.92s   Mean: 9.81s
  p50: 9.36s   p95: 16.15s   p99: 19.52s

Transfer Statistics (n=300):
```

title: Handle parallel Docker image builds in integration tests
body:
Example of failing job: https://github.com/breez/spark-sdk/actions/runs/21964140445/job/63509880164?pr=504

## Summary
This change adds race condition handling to the Docker image build process in integration tests. When multiple tests run in parallel and attempt to build the same Docker image, they can now gracefully handle the "already exists" error instead of failing.

## Key Changes
- **Pre-build check**: Added an upfront `docker image inspect` call to detect if the image already exists before attempting to build, allowing early exit if the image is already available
- **Race condition handling**: Added error handling in the build failure path to catch "already exists" errors that may occur when another parallel test builds the same image during the build process
- **Improved logging**: Added informational log messages to indicate when images are skipped due to existing or being built by another process

## Implementation Details
The solution uses a two-pronged approach:
1. Before building, check if the image already exists using `docker image inspect` and skip the build if it does
2. If the build fails with an "already exists" error in stderr, treat it as a success since another parallel process has already built the image

This prevents test failures in parallel test execution scenarios where multiple tests reference the same Docker image, which is common with Docker buildx.

title: Concurrent claims
body:
This PR introduces two main features to improve SDK performance in high-throughput environments:                                                                          
                                                                                                                                                                            
  - Concurrent Transfer Claims: Adds configurable parallel claiming of pending Spark transfers via the new max_concurrent_claims configuration option                       
  - SyncCoordinator: New abstraction that coalesces duplicate sync requests, reducing redundant sync operations under load                                                  
                                                                                                                                                                            
  **Changes**                                                                                                                                                                   
                                                                                                                                                                            
  Concurrent Claims                                                                                                                                                         
                                                                                                                                                                            
  - Added max_concurrent_claims configuration option to Config (default: 1)                                                                                                 
  - Claims now process in parallel using buffer_unordered when configured with values > 1                                                                                   
  - Failed claims are logged as warnings but don't block other claims (best-effort error handling)                                                                          
  - Useful for server environments with high incoming payment volume                                                                                                        
                                                                                                                                                                            
  **SyncCoordinator**                                                                                                                                                           
                                                                                                                                                                            
  - New SyncCoordinator struct replaces direct sync_trigger usage                                                                                                           
  - Coalesces multiple sync requests of the same type: if requests arrive while a sync is running, they share a single NEW sync that starts after the current one completes 
  - Different sync types are processed in order                                                                                                                             
  - Provides both trigger_sync_and_wait() and trigger_sync_no_wait() methods
  
  **Behavioral** Changes                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                                                                                                            
  Claims now use best-effort error handling: Previously, if any single claim failed, the entire claim_pending_transfers operation would fail. Now, failed claims are logged 
  as warnings and successful claims continue. This is more resilient but callers won't see individual claim errors as exceptions.                                           
                                                                                                                                                                            
  **Configuration**                                                                                                                                                             
                                                                                                                                                                            
 ```

  // For server environments with high payment volume:                                                                                                                      
  config.max_concurrent_claims = 10;
```

title: refactor: group free functions under BreezSdkSpark namespace
body:
### Background
The Breez SDK currently exposes its top-level API as a collection of free functions (`default_config`, `connect`, `parse`, `init_logging`, etc.) and a `BreezSdk` client type. As the SDK matures, this flat structure makes it harder to discover the API surface, creates ambiguity in multi-SDK codebases, and doesn't align well with idiomatic patterns across our target platforms (Rust, Go, Kotlin, Swift, Python, C#, React Native, WASM, Flutter).

This PR establishes a consistent `BreezSdk{Protocol}` namespace and `Breez{Protocol}Client` type convention across all Breez SDK implementations.

---

### What This PR Does

Introduces a `BreezSdkSpark` namespace struct that groups all static/global SDK functions under a single, discoverable entry point:
```rust
// Before
let config = default_config(...);
let sdk: BreezSdk = connect(config, ...)?;

// After
let config = BreezSdkSpark::default_config(...);
let client: BreezSparkClient = BreezSdkSpark::connect(config, ...)?;
```

Adds `BreezSparkClient` as a type alias for `BreezSdk`, enabling a gradual rename without breaking existing code.

Deprecates the existing free functions (since `0.10.0`) with guidance pointing consumers to the `BreezSdkSpark::` equivalents.

### Platform-Specific Namespace Strategy

Each language binding exposes `BreezSdkSpark` idiomatically:

| Platform | Approach | File |
|----------|----------|------|
| **Rust** | `BreezSdkSpark` struct with associated functions | `core/src/app.rs` |
| **WASM** | `#[wasm_bindgen]` struct with static methods | `wasm/src/breez.rs` |
| **Flutter** | `#[frb]` struct with static methods | `flutter/rust/src/breez.rs` |
| **Kotlin** | Hand-written `object BreezSdkSpark` delegating to free functions | `bindings/langs/kotlin-multiplatform/Breez.kt` |
| **Python** | Hand-written class with `@staticmethod` methods | `bindings/langs/python/src/breez_sdk_spark/breez.py` |
| **React Native** | Hand-written TypeScript class with static methods | `packages/react-native/src/Breez.ts` |
| **C#** | `global_methods_class_name = "BreezSdkSpark"` in uniffi.toml | `bindings/uniffi.toml` |
| **Go** | Package name `breez_sdk_spark` serves as namespace naturally | No wrapper needed |
| **Swift** | Module name `BreezSdkSpark` serves as namespace naturally | No wrapper needed |

### Structural Changes for Maintainers

- **`crates/breez-sdk/core/src/app.rs`** — New `BreezSdkSpark` struct with all namespace methods. Split into two impl blocks: one for all targets, one `#[cfg(not(wasm))]` for methods unavailable in WASM.

- **`crates/breez-sdk/core/src/sdk/mod.rs`** — Added `pub type BreezSparkClient = BreezSdk`. All 7 free function deprecation notices updated to `since = "0.10.0"` with `"Use BreezSdkSpark::"` guidance.

- **`crates/breez-sdk/bindings/uniffi.toml`** — Added `global_methods_class_name = "BreezSdkSpark"` under `[bindings.csharp]`

- **`crates/breez-sdk/bindings/Makefile`** — Added `cp` step for `Breez.kt` (Android + KMP targets). Changed C# cleanup pattern from `rm -f *.cs` to `rm -f breez_sdk_spark*.cs` to preserve `global_methods_class_name` output.

- **Hand-written wrapper files** — Kotlin (`Breez.kt`), Python (`breez.py`), React Native (`Breez.ts`) are thin delegation layers that are checked into git alongside `.gitignore` exceptions.

### Why This Approach
- **No breaking changes**. Existing integrations continue to compile and run. Deprecation warnings guide users toward the new API without forcing an immediate migration.
- **Improved discoverability**. Typing `BreezSdkSpark.` in an IDE immediately surfaces all available entry points.
- **Platform consistency.** All 9 platforms get the same `BreezSdkSpark.connect()` / `BreezSdkSpark.defaultConfig()` shape.

### What This Does Not Change
- **Runtime behavior is identical** — `BreezSdkSpark::default_config()` delegates directly to `default_config()` internally.
- **No public API is removed**. Deprecations are warnings only.
- Internal call sites use `#[allow(deprecated)]` to keep CI clean without hiding the signal from external consumers.

- Added a 30-second timeout for balance restoration to complete

title: build: upgrade rusqlite to v0.32
body:
- Bumps `rusqlite` from 0.31.0 to 0.32.1
- Bumps `rusqlite_migration` from 1.2.0 to 1.3.1 (required for rusqlite 0.32 compatibility)

### Motivation

`rusqlite 0.31.0` pulls in `libsqlite3-sys 0.28.0`. Since `libsqlite3-sys` uses `links = "sqlite3"`, Cargo enforces exactly one version of this crate in the final binary. This makes it impossible to compile any project that combines the Spark SDK with crates depending on a newer `libsqlite3-sys`, notably `sqlx-sqlite 0.8.x `(used by `sea-orm`) which requires `libsqlite3-sys ^0.30.1`.

Upgrading to `rusqlite 0.32.1` aligns on `libsqlite3-sys ^0.30.1`, resolving the conflict and allowing the Spark SDK to coexist with the broader Rust database ecosystem.

`sqlx-sqlite` next release `0.9.x` will allow cohabiting with any version between `v0.30,

title: feat: add Python CLI port of Breez SDK Rust CLI
body:
Port the Rust CLI to Python as the first step toward multi-language CLI examples for SDK integrators. Each language port serves as a working reference implementation that exercises the full SDK surface, helping integrators see idiomatic usage patterns in their language.

The Python port mirrors the Rust CLI 1:1, built on the UniFFI Python bindings. Includes a GitHub Actions workflow that uses Claude Code to automatically sync the Python CLI when the Rust source changes.

**Makefile setup (applies to all language CLIs):**

Each CLI has two setup modes, useful when debugging language-specific binding issues:

- `make setup` — builds bindings from local Rust source and installs them. Use this when working on the SDK itself or debugging binding-level issues.
- `make setup-published` — installs the published SDK package (PyPI, Go modules, etc.). Use this to test against a release version.

Both modes share `make run` / `make run-mainnet` for running the CLI. Default network is regtest (free, no API key needed). `make clean` removes build artifacts, virtual environments, and caches. `make help` lists all available targets.

The Go port mirrors the Rust CLI 1:1, built on the UniFFI Go bindings. Includes a per-language sync workflow.

**Go UniFFI notes:**
- `u128` is a type alias for `*big.Int` — so `Amount *u128` means `**big.Int`
- Enum variants use `Field0` for unnamed fields (e.g., `InputTypeLnurlPay.Field0`)
- `*SdkError` is a typed nil on success, needs `liftError()` to convert to untyped nil

Also updates Go doc snippets from v0.1.4-dev2 to v0.10.0.

title: feat: add sync-prompt generator for CLI sync workflows
body:
The binding CLI examples are designed to be self-correcting and LLM-maintained. When the Rust CLI (source of truth) changes, Claude Code automatically syncs the language ports via GitHub Actions, verifies the result compiles, and opens a PR — no human intervention needed for routine changes.

This PR adds the infrastructure that makes this work across languages:

**Sync-prompt generator** — template-based system (similar to snippets-processor) that produces per-language GitHub Actions workflow YAML from a shared prompt template and per-language TOML configs. Adding a new language is just a TOML file.

- `prompt-template.md`: shared Claude Code prompt with `{{PLACEHOLDER}}` markers
- `workflow-template.yml`: GitHub Actions YAML skeleton
- `langs/*.toml`: per-language configs with translation rules, UniFFI quirks, and build check commands
- `generate.py`: reads template + config, writes workflow YAML

**Umbrella workflow** (`sync-cli-langs.yml`) — detects Rust CLI changes on push to main and dispatches per-language sync workflows. Uses `languages-to-sync` input following the `packages-to-publish` pattern from `publish.yml`.

**Diff detection** — each per-lang workflow computes its own diff base: uses `github.event.before` on push events, optional `base-sha` on manual dispatch, or auto-detects from the last commit that touched the language CLI directory via `git log`.

**Build verification** — the sync prompt includes a syntax validation step that Claude must pass before creating a PR (`py_compile` for Python, `gofmt -e` for Go).

**CI check** — `generate.py --check` runs on every PR to prevent generated workflow files from drifting.

title: Fix Python event listener deadlock by auto-setting asyncio event loop
body:
## Summary                                                                                                                         
          
  - Auto-call `uniffi_set_event_loop(asyncio.get_running_loop())` before returning a `BreezSdk` instance from `connect()`, `connect_with_signer()`, and `SdkBuilder.build()`                                                                                  
  - Fixes #662: Python `EventListener.on_event` callbacks deadlock when called from Rust's TOKIO1 thread because it has no Python asyncio event loop

## Context

UniFFI 0.28.3 exports `EventListener.on_event` as an async callback interface. When the SDK emits events from the TOKIO1 background thread (e.g., `TransferClaimed` during sync), UniFFI calls `asyncio.run_coroutine_threadsafe()` which needs a registered event loop. Without one, `asyncio.get_running_loop()` raises `RuntimeError: no running event loop`, the callback never completes, and the Rust `.await` hangs forever — deadlocking `periodic_sync` and all subsequent `sync_wallet()`/`get_info(ensure_synced=True)` calls.

UniFFI provides [uniffi_set_event_loop](https://mozilla.github.io/uniffi-rs/latest/futures.html#python-uniffi_set_event_loop) for this, but users had to call it manually. Since all SDK entry points are `async`, a running event loop is always available — so the SDK can set it automatically.

Verified with glow-cloud.

title: Postgres tree store
body:
**Summary**

Adds a PostgreSQL-backed implementation of the TreeStore trait, enabling persistent tree storage suitable for server-side or multi-instance deployments. This complements the existing in-memory tree store with a durable alternative that supports concurrent access across multiple SDK instances.

**New PostgreSQL Tree Store**                                                                                                                                                                                   
  - Full TreeStore trait implementation backed by PostgreSQL                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  - Database-level concurrency control using advisory locks to prevent deadlocks and leaves race.                                                                                                                                                                                                 
  - Automatic cleanup of stale reservations (5-minute timeout) during set_leaves() to release leaves locked by crashed clients                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
  **Refactored PostgreSQL Module (crates/breez-sdk/core/src/persist/postgres/)**                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  - Split monolithic postgres.rs into modular structure: base.rs, storage.rs, tree_store.rs                                                                                                                                                                                      
  - Extracted common utilities (connection pooling, migrations) into base.rs                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                
**Note**
Also found an issue that refresh may delete recently claimed leaves. Added handling for that in this PR by comparing refresh start timestamp to the store added timetsamp.

Added target to the make file that runs all breez integration tests using postgress store.

title: feat: add Dart CLI port of Breez SDK Rust CLI
body:
Third language port of the Rust CLI, this time to Dart. Dart is the first non-UniFFI language — it uses flutter_rust_bridge (FRB) instead, which exercises the sync infrastructure with a meaningfully different binding layer.

The Dart port mirrors the Rust CLI 1:1, built on the FRB Flutter bindings (`breez_sdk_spark_flutter`). Includes a per-language sync workflow and sync-prompt config.

**Features:**
- All 27 commands + 7 issuer subcommands ported
- Tab completion with prefix matching on all command names
- Persistent command history (up/down arrows, saved across sessions)
- Per-command `help`/`--help` support with usage and option descriptions
- `dart:mirrors`-based serialization for proper JSON output of all FRB types

**Dart/FRB notes:**
- `await BreezSdkSparkLib.init()` must be called once before any SDK usage
- Enum variants are sealed classes with pattern matching (`case SdkEvent_Synced():`) or `is` checks
- Unnamed fields use `.field0` accessor (e.g., `InputType_LnurlPay.field0` is the `LnurlPayRequestDetails`)
- `u128` maps to Dart native `BigInt` — use `BigInt.parse(str)` and `BigInt.from(int)`
- Events come from `sdk.addEventListener()` as a `Stream`, not callbacks
- FRB `Config` is immutable and does NOT have a native `copyWith` method — the CLI provides a custom `ConfigCopyWith` extension in `lib/helpers.dart`
- No `uniffi_set_event_loop` needed — Dart handles async natively
- `SdkBuilder` methods consume the builder — must capture return value (`builder = builder.withDefaultStorage(...)`)
- `exit(0)` required after `runCli()` because native FFI keeps background threads alive after `sdk.disconnect()`

The Swift port mirrors the Rust CLI 1:1, built on the UniFFI Swift bindings. Includes a per-language sync workflow and sync-prompt config.

title: improve query performance
body:
The LNURL server was logging slow query warnings:                                                                                  

```
  WARN sqlx::query: slow statement: execution time exceeded alert threshold
    elapsed=3.674929167s slow_threshold=1s

    SELECT ph.payment_hash
    ,      sc.sender_comment
    ,      z.zap_request
    ,      z.zap_event
    ,      GREATEST(COALESCE(z.updated_at, 0), COALESCE(sc.updated_at, 0),
                    COALESCE(i.updated_at, 0)) AS updated_at
    ,      i.preimage
    FROM (
        SELECT payment_hash FROM invoices WHERE user_pubkey = $1
        UNION
        SELECT payment_hash FROM zaps WHERE user_pubkey = $1
        UNION
        SELECT payment_hash FROM sender_comments WHERE user_pubkey = $1
    ) ph
    LEFT JOIN invoices i ON ph.payment_hash = i.payment_hash
    LEFT JOIN zaps z ON ph.payment_hash = z.payment_hash
    LEFT JOIN sender_comments sc ON ph.payment_hash = sc.payment_hash
    WHERE GREATEST(COALESCE(z.updated_at, 0), COALESCE(sc.updated_at, 0),
                   COALESCE(i.updated_at, 0)) > $4
    ORDER BY updated_at ASC
    OFFSET $2 LIMIT $3
```

  This also caused connection pool exhaustion (acquired connection, but time to acquire exceeded slow threshold, 14.8s wait), since
  the slow query held connections for too long.

  Root cause

  The UNION subquery scanned all payment hashes for a user regardless of updated_at, then the WHERE GREATEST(...) filter was applied
  after 3 LEFT JOINs — processing the entire history just to filter it down.

  Fix

  1. Pushed updated_at > $4 filter into each UNION leg — the UNION now only collects recently-updated payment hashes, making the set
  proportional to the delta instead of full history. This also makes the WHERE GREATEST(...) clause redundant (if any table has
  updated_at > $4, the UNION includes it; the LEFT JOINs still fetch full data by PK).
  2. Added composite indexes (user_pubkey, updated_at) on invoices, zaps, and sender_comments — each UNION leg now does an efficient
  index range scan.

title: refactor: single matrix workflow for CLI sync
body:
## Summary

- Replace 4 generated per-language workflow files + parent dispatcher + workflow template with a single `sync-cli.yml` using matrix strategy (~1,800 lines → ~400 lines)
- Move git/PR operations from the Claude prompt into workflow steps — Claude focuses on code changes only, workflow handles branch creation, commit, push, and PR
- Add per-language runners: Swift gets `macos-latest` (needs protoc + Rust for `check-swift`), others use `ubuntu-latest`
- Add per-language timeouts: Swift gets 45m (Rust build), others 30m
- Reuse existing open sync PRs: fixed branch names (`claude/sync--cli`), force-push + `gh pr edit` if PR exists
- Remove SHA from concurrency groups — newer pushes cancel in-progress syncs
- Simplify `allowed_tools` — removed git/gh permissions (Claude no longer needs them)
- Add Python CLI static analysis (`check-python`) to CI
- Increase Claude log artifact retention from 7 to 30 days
- Conditional `fetch-depth`: shallow clone (1) for approve flow, full clone (0) otherwise
- Structured PR body with `` for sync findings, source commit link, Claude log link, `CLI` label
- Structured findings template (`## Divergences` / `## Applied` / `## Skipped`) in prompt
- Auto-assign last Rust CLI committer as PR reviewer (skips bot accounts)

Addresses items from #706: §1 (log retention), §2 (duplicate PRs), §3 (concurrency + structured PR body), §5 (structured findings), §6 (auto-assign reviewer), §7 (scalability/matrix + timeouts).

## Test plan

- [ ] Run `python generate.py --prompt-only dart` locally — verify prompt renders correctly
- [ ] Trigger `sync-cli.yml` with `dry-run=true` for a single language
- [ ] Verify matrix expands correctly (4 jobs, Swift on macOS)
- [ ] Verify PR reuse: trigger twice on same language, confirm second run updates existing PR
- [ ] Verify language filtering: `languages=dart,go` runs only 2 jobs
- [ ] Verify reviewer is resolved from last Rust CLI committer (check workflow notice log)
- [ ] Verify bot committers are skipped for reviewer assignment

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: Upgrade to UniFFI 0.29
body:
Closes #495

Test publish run https://github.com/breez/spark-sdk/actions/runs/22769799183
Published as `0.11.0-dev2`

Did some manual smoke tests on Go, Go on iOS, KMP and RN/Expo projects.

## Summary

Upgrade UniFFI from 0.28 to 0.29 across all binding generators and language targets.

### Core changes

- **uniffi**: 0.28.3 → 0.29.5 (Breez fork `fix-python-async-protocol-v0.29.5` — carries the Python async protocol fix, plain `def` instead of `async def` for callback methods)
- **Custom type syntax**: Migrated `u128` custom type converter from the deprecated `UniffiCustomTypeConverter` trait to the new declarative `uniffi::custom_type!` macro with `remote` flag

### Binding generators updated

| Generator | Old | New |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **uniffi-bindgen-cs** (C#) | `jssdwt-fix-async-event-listener` | `v0.29.4-with-fixes` |
| **gobley** (Kotlin/KMP) | rev `7275391...` | `v0.3.7-with-fixes` |
| **uniffi-bindgen-go** | rev `8b8c58f...` | `v0.29.5-with-fixes` |
| **uniffi-bindgen-react-native** | `0.28.3-5` | `0.29.3-1` |


### C# snippets

- Updated to use arrays (`T[]`) instead of `List` for UniFFI-generated types (breaking change in C# bindings for 0.29)

### React Native

- Replaced the large 492-line patch for `uniffi-bindgen-react-native` with a much smaller 58-line patch (only the Android `cpp-adapter.cpp` JNI fix and iOS podspec source_files fix remain)
- Upgraded podspec name to `BreezSdkSparkReactNative` (PascalCase)
- Added `proguard-rules.pro` for Android
- Added `uniffiInitAsync()` no-op export for parity with the web version
- Simplified `CMakeLists.txt` path resolution using `get_filename_component`
- Updated CI workflow to include `patches/` and `proguard-rules.pro` in artifacts

### Swift

- Removed the `UniffiForeignFutureTask` protocol redeclaration workaround (fixed upstream in UniFFI 0.29, see [mozilla/uniffi-rs#2341](https://github.com/mozilla/uniffi-rs/pull/2341))

## Breaking changes
Vecs are now mapped to C# arrays instead of lists

title: chore: sync Python CLI with Rust CLI changes
body:
Automated sync of Python CLI from Rust CLI changes.

**Source commit:** [`708415e`](https://github.com/breez/spark-sdk/commit/708415e71e2174b675503ab9612f3f399bf5610c)
**Claude log:** [View artifact](https://github.com/breez/spark-sdk/actions/runs/22794468076)


Sync findings

## Divergences
- Rust CLI added `--passkey`, `--wallet-name`, `--list-wallet-names`, `--store-wallet-name`, `--rpid` CLI flags — all missing from Python CLI
- Rust CLI added `passkey/` module with `PasskeyProvider` enum, `PasskeyConfig`, `resolve_passkey_seed()` orchestration, and three PRF providers (file, yubikey, fido2) — entirely missing from Python CLI
- Rust `main.rs` conditionally derives seed from passkey provider or mnemonic — Python `main.py` always used mnemonic
- Rust README added passkey documentation section with provider details and setup instructions — Python README had no passkey section

## Applied
- Created `passkey.py` with `FilePrfProvider` (fully portable HMAC-SHA256), `YubiKeyPrfProvider` and `Fido2PrfProvider` skeletons, `create_provider()` factory, and `resolve_passkey_seed()` orchestration using SDK types (`Passkey`, `NostrRelayConfig`, `PasskeyPrfProvider`)
- Updated `main.py` to add `--passkey`, `--wallet-name`, `--list-wallet-names`, `--store-wallet-name`, `--rpid` CLI options with validation matching Rust's `clap` constraints
- Updated `main.py` to conditionally derive seed from passkey provider or fall back to mnemonic
- Updated `README.md` CLI Options table with all five new passkey flags
- Updated `README.md` with Passkey section documenting file provider usage and noting yubikey/fido2 as not yet available

## Skipped
- **YubiKey PRF provider**: Requires USB HID access to YubiKey for HMAC-SHA1 challenge-response (Slot 2). The Rust CLI uses the `challenge-response` crate (wraps `yubico-manager`). Python package `yubikey-manager` (`ykman`) exists on PyPI but is a CLI management tool, not a challenge-response library. No well-known Python package provides the HMAC-SHA1 OTP challenge-response protocol needed. Implemented as a skeleton that prints "not yet supported" and exits.
- **FIDO2 PRF provider**: Requires USB HID access to FIDO2 authenticator for CTAP2 hmac-secret extension. The Rust CLI uses the `ctap-hid-fido2` crate. Python packages `fido2` (from Yubico) on PyPI could potentially implement this, but the hmac-secret PRF extension usage is non-trivial and would need careful integration. Implemented as a skeleton that prints "not yet supported" and exits.
